provenance.source: IAU 2015 nominal solar radius / NASA Sun Fact Sheet

render_approximations.what: emissive sphere + point light with no distance falloff (decay 0); no limb darkening, no rotation, no corona
render_approximations.why: what matters at rung 3 is that light comes FROM the Sun's true position — Earth's terminator and the Moon's phases now follow from geometry, not from a hardcoded lamp direction
render_approximations.correction_path: physical solar rendering later; light *direction* is already correct, which is the honest part
render_approximations.expected_error: visual only — illumination direction is exact

orbit._angles_note: inclination is 0 by DEFINITION — the ecliptic is Earth's orbital plane (J2000); longitude of perihelion 102.947° carried in ω with Ω=0

provenance.source: NASA Earth Fact Sheet (mean volumetric radius; orbit: J2000 mean elements)
provenance.note: radius is the mean; Earth is an oblate spheroid (equatorial 6378.1 km, polar 6356.8 km) — sphere rendering is a knowing simplification

render_approximations.what: Keplerian two-body ellipse with FIXED mean elements — no perihelion precession, no planetary perturbations
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides
render_approximations.expected_error: small vs true ephemeris at this rung's purposes; unquantified — honest residual, to be measured against Horizons before being trusted further

orbit._angles_note: Omega and omega are J2000 mean values and PRECESS (nodal ~18.6 yr retrograde, apsidal ~8.85 yr prograde) — held fixed here, see render_approximations

provenance.source: NASA Moon Fact Sheet

distance_provenance.how: round-trip light time to retroreflectors (Apollo 11/14/15, Lunokhod 1/2); millimeter precision (APOLLO experiment)
distance_provenance.note: rung 0 of the cosmic distance ladder — geometric, no astrophysical model in the loop (only relativistic light-time corrections). Bonus measured fact: LLR shows the Moon receding at ~3.8 cm/yr.
distance_provenance.instantaneous_range_km: ~356,500–406,700 over long-term extremes; WIDER than the fixed-element a(1∓e) = 363,296/405,504 because of solar perturbations — consistent with the fixed-elements residual labeled in render_approximations
distance_provenance.credit: folded in from Researches/cosmic-distance-measurement via PCLA draft (termux-claude-907815e6, 2026-07-19)

render_approximations.what: Keplerian two-body ellipse with FIXED mean elements — no apsidal/nodal precession, no perturbations (evection, variation); rendered relative to the moving Earth
render_approximations.why: rung 2 rendered the true ellipse shape and tilt; the remaining gap is that the real Moon is heavily perturbed by the Sun and its ellipse rotates
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides — real positions, not elements
render_approximations.expected_error: up to ~5,000 km positional vs true ephemeris (lunar perturbation terms) — labeled, not hidden

retired.what: orbit drawn circular at semi-major axis, in the ecliptic plane

provenance.source: NASA Mercury Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements — notably omits Mercury's famous perihelion precession (~5,600 arcsec/century, incl. the 43"/century GR term)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides
render_approximations.expected_error: grows secularly; fine for a labeled orrery, useless for prediction — that asymmetry is the label's whole job

provenance.source: NASA Venus Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; the nearly-circular orbit (e=0.0068) makes the residual smallest of any planet
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides

provenance.source: NASA Mars Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; Mars's high eccentricity (0.0935) makes its aphelion/perihelion asymmetry visible in the rendered ellipse
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides

provenance.source: NASA Jupiter Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; the four Galilean moons now orbit as child bodies, but the ~90 smaller moons and the ring system are omitted; sphere ignores the ~6.5% oblateness
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides; oblate spheroid geometry; remaining satellites + rings

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0: the orbit is near-circular (e=0.004) and precesses fast under Jupiter's oblateness, so these angles are weakly determined and physically un-pinned in a fixed-element model — they only rotate an almost-round ellipse and do not move the perihelion/aphelion the self-check tests

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Jovian satellite mean elements; radius from Galileo/Voyager imaging
provenance.note: One of the four moons Galileo saw on 1610-01-07 and named the Medicean Stars — four points of light circling something that was not Earth, the observation that broke the geocentric sky. Innermost Galilean and the most volcanically active body known: tidal heating, pumped by the Io:Europa:Ganymede 1:2:4 Laplace resonance, keeps it molten. That resonance is not put in by hand here — it falls out of the three measured periods (ratios 2.01 and 4.04).

render_approximations.what: elements are referenced to Jupiter's Laplace/equatorial plane but applied directly in the ecliptic frame: the ~3.1° tilt of that plane to Jupiter's orbit (and its node) is omitted, so the moon plane is drawn nearly flat to the ecliptic — off by a few degrees, invisible at this scale. Fixed two-body ellipse, no precession.
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Jupiter's pole orientation (Laplace plane → ecliptic); JPL Horizons for precessing elements

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0 for the same reason as Io: near-circular, fast-precessing, weakly pinned

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Jovian satellite mean elements; radius from Galileo imaging
provenance.note: Second Galilean. Smoothest surface in the solar system — a young ice shell over a saltwater ocean holding more liquid water than all of Earth's, kept warm by the same resonant tidal flexing. The middle rung of the 1:2:4 chain.

render_approximations.what: Laplace-plane elements applied in the ecliptic frame (~3.1° tilt + node omitted); fixed two-body ellipse, no precession
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Jupiter's pole; JPL Horizons

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0: near-circular (e=0.001), weakly pinned

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Jovian satellite mean elements; radius from Galileo imaging
provenance.note: Third Galilean and the largest moon in the solar system — 2634 km radius, bigger than the planet Mercury (2440 km). The only moon with its own magnetosphere. Outer rung of the 1:2:4 resonance.

render_approximations.what: Laplace-plane elements applied in the ecliptic frame (~3.1° tilt + node omitted); fixed two-body ellipse, no precession
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Jupiter's pole; JPL Horizons

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0: near-circular, weakly pinned

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Jovian satellite mean elements; radius from Galileo imaging
provenance.note: Outermost Galilean and the most heavily cratered body known — an ancient, dead surface that never resurfaced. It sits OUTSIDE the 1:2:4 resonance, which is exactly why it stayed cold and geologically still while the inner three are heated: the same measured periods that place Io, Europa and Ganymede in lockstep leave Callisto free.

render_approximations.what: Laplace-plane elements applied in the ecliptic frame (~3.1° tilt + node omitted); fixed two-body ellipse, no precession
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Jupiter's pole; JPL Horizons

provenance.source: NASA Saturn Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; NO RINGS yet — Saturn without rings is a knowing omission, not an oversight; ~9.8% oblateness ignored
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons; ring geometry (measured: ~7,000–80,000 km above equator) as its own labeled arrival

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0: the orbit precesses under Saturn's oblateness and these angles are weakly pinned in a fixed-element model — they rotate the ellipse but do not move the perihelion/aphelion the self-check tests

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Saturnian satellite mean elements; radius from Cassini radar/imaging
provenance.note: Second-largest moon in the solar system, and the only one with a substantial atmosphere — ~1.5 bar of nitrogen, thicker than Earth's, wrapped in an orange organic haze that hid its surface until Cassini's radar and the Huygens probe (landed 2005-01-14, the most distant landing ever made). It has a full liquid cycle like Earth's, but the rain, rivers and polar seas are methane and ethane, not water. Discovered by Christiaan Huygens in 1655.

render_approximations.what: elements referenced to Saturn's Laplace/equatorial plane are applied directly in the ecliptic frame: the plane's tilt to Saturn's orbit (and its node) is omitted, so the orbit is drawn a few degrees off — invisible at this scale; fixed two-body ellipse, no precession; the orange atmospheric haze is not rendered (the sphere is a bare ~2575 km point)
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Saturn's pole orientation; JPL Horizons for precessing elements

provenance.source: NASA Uranus Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; the 97.8° axial tilt is not rendered (no rotation shown at all yet)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons; axial tilt + rotation when spin becomes visible (textures)

provenance.source: NASA Neptune Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)
provenance.note: the planet found by arithmetic before telescope — Le Verrier's residuals in Uranus's orbit were a labeled anomaly that got RESOLVED, the founding legend of this whole project

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements (Neptune has completed barely one orbit since discovery — mean elements are least tested here)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides

orbit._angles_note: node and periapsis set to 0: the orbit is essentially a perfect circle (e=0.000016), so periapsis is undefined and node weakly pinned — neither moves the a(1∓e) the self-check tests

provenance.source: NASA/JPL Neptunian satellite mean elements; radius from Voyager 2 (1989 flyby)
provenance.note: The one moon that tells you it doesn't belong: inclination 156.9° means Triton orbits Neptune BACKWARDS (retrograde), against the planet's spin. Large moons that formed with their planet all go prograde — so Triton was CAPTURED, a Kuiper Belt object Neptune caught and never let go, its orbit the fossil of that violence. It is the coldest measured surface in the solar system (~38 K) yet geologically alive: Voyager 2 photographed nitrogen geysers erupting through its cantaloupe-textured ice. Tidal drag is spiralling it inward; in a few billion years Neptune will tear it into a ring. Discovered by William Lassell in 1846, just 17 days after Neptune itself.

render_approximations.what: elements referenced to Neptune's Laplace/equatorial plane applied directly in the ecliptic frame (plane tilt + node omitted); fixed two-body circle, no precession; nitrogen geysers and cantaloupe terrain not rendered (bare ~1353 km sphere)
render_approximations.correction_path: full frame transform through Neptune's pole orientation; JPL Horizons

provenance.source: JPL Small-Body Database; radius from Dawn orbiter (2015–2018) — spacecraft-grade
provenance.note: first asteroid ever discovered (Piazzi, 1801-01-01), then LOST behind the Sun and recovered because Gauss invented modern orbit determination to find it again — the founding legend of fitting orbits to short arcs. Promoted to dwarf planet in the same IAU 2006 resolution that demoted Pluto: one vote, one label moved down, one up.

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements (osculating elements at a recent epoch; main-belt perturbations by Jupiter ignored)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides

provenance.source: NASA Pluto Fact Sheet / Standish J2000 mean elements; radius from New Horizons (2015)
provenance.note: a DWARF planet — reclassified by IAU vote in 2006; the reclassification changed the label, not the orbit. Discovered 1930: with a 248-year period, humanity has watched barely a third of one Pluto orbit — its mean elements rest on the shortest observed arc of any body here except the conjecture.

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; omits the 2:3 Neptune resonance that actually PROTECTS Pluto (mean elements make the orbits look like they could collide near the node crossing — the resonance phase-locks them so they never do)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides, where the resonance is implicit in the positions
render_approximations.expected_error: the crossing-orbits impression is the misleading part, not the km error — a label about geometry creating a false STORY, not just false positions

provenance.source: JPL Small-Body Database (1P/Halley), J2000 osculating elements; nucleus size + shape from the Giotto flyby (ESA, 1986)
provenance.note: The founding triumph of the DERIVED tier: Edmond Halley applied Newton's brand-new gravitation to the comets of 1531/1607/1682, argued they were one returning body, and PREDICTED its return for 1758. He died in 1742 and never saw it — but it came back on schedule. This orbit is why 'derived' is trustworthy: a man bet his name across his own death on the equations and won. Retrograde (i>90°): Halley orbits the Sun backwards. Last perihelion 1986; next predicted 2061-07-28 — itself a falsifiable derived claim.

render_approximations.what: fixed osculating elements; NON-GRAVITATIONAL forces omitted — a comet's sublimation jets thrust it off the pure-gravity path by thousands of km per apparition (why Halley's return dates drift and must be re-fit each orbit)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons with non-gravitational parameters (A1, A2, A3)
render_approximations.expected_error: coma and tail are not rendered (decorative); the nucleus is a true-scale ~5.5 km point — invisible beside its own orbit, which is the honest picture

provenance.source: JPL Small-Body Database (C/1995 O1), post-1997-perihelion osculating elements; nucleus diameter ~30 km from HST + radio (Weaver/Fernández)
provenance.note: The stress test that upgraded the solver. At e=0.995086 this orbit is near-parabolic: perihelion 0.916 AU, aphelion 371.9 AU. The page's original undamped-Newton Kepler solver DIVERGED here (worst residual 6.75e11 rad vs the <1e-9 gate) — the decidable self-check refused it, exactly as designed. It ships only after a safeguarded-Newton solver (bracketed [M-e,M+e] with bisection fallback) was proven to converge on every body PLUS this one (worst residual 2.0e-14 rad). Jupiter's 1997 passage cut the period from ~4200 to ~2500 yr; the elements here are the post-encounter osculating set, not the incoming orbit.

render_approximations.what: fixed osculating elements; non-gravitational forces and the Jupiter-altered pre/post-1997 orbit split are both collapsed into a single post-encounter element set
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons two-epoch fit (incoming vs outgoing orbit)
render_approximations.expected_error: coma and tail not rendered (decorative); nucleus is a true-scale ~30 km point

provenance.source: Batygin & Brown 2021, 'The Orbit of Planet Nine' (AJ) — a≈380 AU (+140/−80), e≈0.21, i≈16°, m≈6.2 M⊕; inferred from the clustering of distant Kuiper-belt object orbits
provenance.note: EVERY number here is a posterior estimate, not a measurement — including the radius (derived from ~6 M⊕ assuming ice-giant composition; nobody has ever seen this object). The orbit shown is the distribution's peak; the real uncertainty is enormous.
provenance.epoch_note: the rendered orbit is the BB2021 peak and is already DATED: Pan-STARRS1+ZTF+DES exclude ~78% of the BB2021 parameter space (arXiv:2401.17977), and later posteriors moved the peak (a≈460 +160/−100 AU update; a≈290±30 AU in a 2025 estimate, arXiv:2410.18170). Conjectured tier tolerates a dated peak — but only if it says so. This is it saying so. [cross-operator review: PCLA termux-claude-907815e6, 2026-07-19]
provenance.falsifier: The kill is a CONJUNCTION — neither leg alone suffices (LSST's plan misses part of the northern swath and leaves a large fraction of the posterior too faint even at full depth; the 2021 selection-bias claim was itself rebutted on survey-sensitivity grounds): (1) most of the surviving parameter space excluded by surveys AND (2) the enlarged Rubin TNO sample shows NO orbital clustering → demotion-by-append. A detection (optical, or the thermal-IR route — far-IR surveys like IRAS/AKARI cross-matching, arXiv:2504.17288 — is a second, independent family) promotes it to measured. Either outcome is the system working.

render_approximations.what: single best-fit orbit drawn for what is really a broad posterior distribution; rendered ghosted + dashed so conjecture can never be mistaken for measurement
render_approximations.correction_path: on detection: real elements; on falsification: removal-by-append

provenance.source: Gaia DR3 (parallax 768.0665 mas → 4.2465 ly); RA/Dec J2000; radius 0.1542 R☉ from interferometry
provenance.note: The first star in the sky, and the nearest one to the Sun — a red dwarf 4.25 light-years away. Its DISTANCE is the point of this rung: measured by trigonometric parallax, rung 1 of the cosmic distance ladder, the step directly above the Moon's laser ranging (rung 0). Parallax is pure geometry — the star appears to shift against the far background as Earth swings to opposite sides of its orbit; the shift's half-angle and the known Earth–Sun baseline give the distance by triangle alone, no astrophysical model in the loop. Founding case: Friedrich Bessel measured 61 Cygni this way in 1838 — the first time a star's distance was known — the parallax equivalent of Halley's derived-tier bet. Proxima's own parallax (0.768″) is the largest of any star, which is exactly what 'nearest' means.

distance_provenance.how: annual parallax 768.07 mas (Gaia DR3): the star's apparent ellipse against the background as Earth orbits; distance = baseline / tan(parallax), geometry only
distance_provenance.note: rung 1 of the cosmic distance ladder — the first purely-geometric rung beyond the solar system, sitting directly above the Moon's LLR (rung 0). Both rungs share the honesty that no astrophysical model sits in the loop: LLR times light, parallax turns a triangle. Everything past a few thousand parsecs needs the model-laden rungs above this one (standard candles, redshift) — this is the last one you can walk by geometry alone.

render_approximations.what: POSITION IS NOT TO SCALE. The rendered direction is real (RA/Dec J2000 → ecliptic frame, obliquity 23.4393°), but the star is drawn on the decorative star-shell (2.5×10¹¹ km) because its true distance, 4.02×10¹³ km, is 8,898× Neptune's orbit and 67× beyond the camera's far clip plane — it cannot be placed to scale in a solar-system view. The marker's size is not to scale either (a true 107,000 km disc would be far sub-pixel). Only the DIRECTION and the stated distance are measured; the drawn radius and radial position are the approximation.
render_approximations.correction_path: none possible in a single-scale scene — the gap IS the datum the distance ladder exists to cross; a true-scale view would need a logarithmic-radius or multi-scale renderer (a future rung)

provenance.note: sim day 0 puts EVERY body at periapsis simultaneously — an alignment that never happens on any real calendar date. Correction path: epoch-referenced mean anomalies, then Horizons.

random points for depth perception — NOT real star positions. As of rung 6 exactly ONE real star sits among them in its true direction: Proxima Centauri (a separate 'measured' object) — the boundary between scenery and sky, drawn on the same shell. Correction path: Hipparcos/Gaia bright-star catalog for the rest.
